1: empneōn apeilēs kai phonou (empneōn apeilēs kai phonou, 'breathing out threats and murder'): empneō (breathe out) applied to threats makes Saul's hostility atmospheric — violence is his very breath. Luke's physicality intensifies the contrast with the new breath (pnoē) of the Spirit at Pentecost. Paul's retrospective self-description ('beyond measure I persecuted', Galatians 1:13) matches Luke's characterization.
2: hodos (hē Hodos, 'the Way'): Acts' preferred self-designation for the Christian movement (Acts 9:2; Acts 19:9, 23; Acts 22:4; Acts 24:14, 22). The term may derive from Jesus's 'I am the Way' (John 14:6) or from Isaiah 40:3's 'way in the desert'; it presents the community as those traveling a specific path rather than holding a particular ideology — discipleship as direction of life. tēs synagōgēs grammata: letters to synagogues assert the high priest's judicial jurisdiction over diaspora Jews.
3: exaiphnēs te auton periēstrapsen phōs ek tou ouranou ('suddenly a light from heaven flashed around him'): exaiphnēs (suddenly, immediately) marks the theophanic disruption; periēstrapsen (flashed around) describes the surrounding light of divine presence. The Damascus theophany will be narrated three times in Acts (9, 22, 26) — each retelling emphasizes different aspects for different audiences. Luke's frequency signals its foundational importance for the Gentile mission.
4: Saoul Saoul ti me diōkeis ('Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?'): the double-name vocative is the biblical marker of divine address at decisive moments (Abraham in Genesis 22:11; Moses in Exodus 3:4; Samuel in 1 Samuel 3:10). The Hebrew form Saoul (not the Greek Saulos) suggests the voice addressed him in his own language — confirmed in 22:7 and Acts 26:14 (Hebrew/Aramaic). The question 'why do you persecute me?' identifies the community's persecution with Christ's own.
5: ego eimi Iēsous hon sy diōkeis ('I am Jesus whom you are persecuting'): the identification formula ego eimi (I am) echoes the divine self-disclosure of Exodus 3:14 and the Johannine Christological formula. The risen Christ's identification of himself with his persecuted followers (not 'my followers' but 'me') is the foundation for the church's theology of suffering — to suffer for Christ is to suffer with Christ (Colossians 1:24; 2 Corinthians 1:5).
6: alla anastēthi kai eiselthe eis tēn polin ('but rise and enter the city'): anastēthi (rise) — the same imperative as resurrection proclamation — is the first word Saul hears from Jesus. He is directed to the city where he planned to arrest believers; the city becomes the place of his own transformation. The passive reception ('you will be told') keeps the initiative entirely divine.
7: hestekeisan de enneoī hoi andres hoi synoiporountes ('the men traveling with him stood speechless'): enneoi (speechless, struck dumb) is a rare word for the stupor of shock. The companions present a corroboration problem: 22:9 says they saw the light but did not hear the voice; 9:7 says they heard the sound but saw no one. The different objects of 'hear' (phōnē) suggest they heard a sound without understanding the articulate speech Saul received.
8: oiden oun Saulos apo tēs gēs ōnewgmenōn de tōn ophthalmōn autou ouden eblepēn ('Saul got up from the ground; when his eyes were opened he could see nothing'): the open eyes that see nothing are the paradox of the encounter — the persecutor who thought he saw clearly is now physically blind. Three days without sight or food mirrors the three days of the resurrection; Saul's transformation has a cruciform structure: the old Saul must die before the new Paul can rise.
9: kai ēn hēmeras treis mē blepōn kai ouk ephagen oud' epien ('for three days he was blind and neither ate nor drank'): the three-day fast parallels Jonah's three days (Jonah 1:17) and the resurrection's three-day pattern. The blindness removes all of Saul's previous competencies — he who pursued with authority is now led by the hand; the man who destroyed others' communities must now be cared for. The fast signals the gravity of the transition.
10: mathētēs de tis ēn en Damasko onomati Hananias ('there was a disciple in Damascus named Ananias'): Ananias is identified only as mathētēs (disciple) — not an apostle, not a prominent figure, but an ordinary member of the Damascus community. The Lord speaks to him in a vision (en horamati), using the same address-form as to Saul — the divine initiative reaches both persecutor and ordinary believer simultaneously.
11: Saulos gar idou proseuchetai ('for behold, Saul is praying'): the word the Lord gives Ananias as reassurance is that Saul is praying — the one who came to arrest those who call on the name is himself now calling on the name. The idou (behold) marks this as surprising evidence: the persecutor in prayer is the visible sign of his transformation before any baptism or anointing.
12: kai eiden andra onomati Hananias eiselthonta kai epithenta autō cheiras ('and he has seen a man named Ananias come and place hands on him'): the double-vision structure (Saul's vision of Ananias, Ananias's vision of the Lord) interlocks to confirm the divine coordination of the encounter. The Lord has prepared both parties simultaneously; neither is acting on their own discernment alone.
13: kyrie, ēkousa apo pollōn peri tou andros toutou ('Lord, I have heard from many about this man'): Ananias's hesitation is presented without editorial criticism — it is reasonable protest based on real intelligence. The community's experience of Saul's persecution is the valid ground for Ananias's fear; the Lord's response does not rebuke the hesitation but overrides it with a larger purpose.
14: tous epikaloumenous to onoma sou ('those who call on your name'): the community is defined by its practice of calling on the name — the Joel-quotation (Acts 2:21) made 'calling on the name of the Lord' the salvation criterion; the Damascus disciples are those who do this. Saul came to arrest those who call on the name; the Lord who is called upon is sending him back to serve the community he persecuted.
15: skeuos eklogēs estin moi houtos ('this man is my chosen instrument'): skeuos (vessel, instrument) is used for functional objects; Paul becomes in Acts the vessel through which the mission extends to Gentiles, kings, and Israel. Eklogē (election, choice) grounds the appointment in divine prerogative — Paul will later describe his conversion as grace 'from my mother's womb' (Galatians 1:15-16). The three-part scope (Gentiles, kings, Israel) matches Acts' narrative structure through Acts 28.
16: dei gar auton hypodeixai hosa dei auton hyper tou onomatos mou pathein ('for I will show him how much he must suffer for my name'): the repeated dei (it is necessary) frames Saul's future suffering as divinely ordained — the same necessity that governed Jesus's own passion (Luke 9:22; Acts 22:37). The persecutor will become the persecuted; the man who made others suffer for the name will himself suffer for the name. Paul's theology of the cross is grounded in this commission.
17: adelphe Saoul ('Brother Saul'): Ananias's address is the decisive social act of reconciliation — the member of the community that Saul came to arrest calls him brother before the healing or baptism. The brotherhood precedes the restoration of sight; the community's embrace is the first sign of the transformation the Lord announced. The address echoes Peter's adelphoi to the Pentecost crowd.
18: hōsei lepides apo tōn ophthalmōn autou ('something like scales from his eyes'): lepides (scales, flakes) is a medical or descriptive term for the film or crust that covered sightless eyes; the imagery of scales falling away is vivid and physical. anablepsas (seeing again): the ana- prefix indicates the restoration of what was lost — not new sight but returned sight. Baptism follows immediately; the sequence (sight → baptism → food → strength) marks a complete restoration.
19: egeneto meta tōn en Damaskō mathētōn hēmeras tinas ('he spent some days with the disciples in Damascus'): Saul's first post-conversion community is the community he came to destroy. The 'some days' in Damascus before his synagogue preaching (Acts 9:20) and his later Arabian withdrawal (Galatians 1:17) are reconciled if the Galatians period comes between the Damascus days and the Jerusalem visit.
20: eutheōs en tais synagōgais ekērysso ton Iēsoun ('immediately in the synagogues he was proclaiming Jesus'): eutheōs (immediately) is Luke's signal for Spirit-driven urgency; no apprenticeship intervenes. The content is specified as proclaiming Jesus — the same object as Philip's Samaritan mission (Acts 9:5) — but now from one who knew the OT scripturally as well as Ananias's community did experientially.
21: pantes de hoi akouontes existanto kai elegon ('all who heard were astonished and said'): the astonishment (existanto) is the same word used for Simon Magus's effect on the Samaritans (Acts 8:9, 11); the transformation of the destroyer into a proclaimer produces the same shock as a miracle. symbibazōn (demonstrating, proving): the verb means to bring together evidence to form a conclusion — Saul's method is demonstrative proof, not just assertion.
22: pleiō de mallon enedunamōto ('he grew more and more powerful'): enedunamoō (strengthen, empower) is the Pauline growth verb (Philippians 4:13; 2 Timothy 4:17); the same power that Saul was trying to suppress in the church now characterizes his own ministry. symbibazōn hoti houtos estin ho Christos ('proving that this is the Messiah'): the same symbibazōn as Acts 9:22 — Saul is an exegetical demonstrator, reasoning from texts.
23: hōs de eplērounto hēmerai hikanai ('when many days had passed'): the time-gap accommodates Paul's Galatians account of the Arabian withdrawal (Galatians 1:17); Luke compresses a period that Paul describes as significant. synebouleusanto oi Ioudaioi anelein auton ('the Jews plotted to kill him'): the pattern will repeat throughout Acts — the same communities that opposed the apostles before Paul now oppose Paul. The persecutor suffers the persecution he inflicted.
24: pareterounton de kai tas pylas hēmeras te kai nyktos hopōs auton anelōsin (they were watching the city gates day and night to kill him): the continuous pareterounton (keeping watch) shows the siege-mentality of the plot. Paul's account in 2 Corinthians 11:32 adds that the ethnarch of Aretas was also involved — a confluence of Jewish and Nabatean opposition. The basket escape (Acts 9:25) is one of Paul's most remembered humiliations (2 Corinthians 11:33).
25: labontes de hoi mathētai autou nyktos dia tou teichous kathēkan auton chalasantes en spyridi ('his disciples lowered him through the wall at night in a basket'): spyris is a large wicker or rope basket; the escape method is both practical and ironic — the zealot who came bearing arrest-letters is smuggled out like cargo. The community (now hoi mathētai autou, 'his disciples') that he came to destroy now protects him.
26: peirāto kollasthai tois mathētais; kai pantes ephobounto auton ('he tried to join the disciples, but all were afraid of him'): kollaomai (attach oneself to, associate with) is the social-bonding verb; the Jerusalem community's fear is entirely understandable. The irony continues: Saul whose letters authorized the arrest of believers is now himself treated as a security threat by the community he wants to join.
27: Barnabas de epilabomenos auton ēgagen pros tous apostolous ('but Barnabas took hold of him and brought him to the apostles'): epilambanomai (take hold of, grasp) implies an initiative Barnabas took without being asked. Barnabas (named 'son of encouragement' in Acts 4:36) becomes the advocate whose personal vouching enables Saul's reception. The report he gives — Damascus road event, Damascus preaching boldly — establishes the apostolic authentication Saul needed.
28: eisporeuomenos kai ekporeuomenos eis Ierousalēm parrēsiazomenos en tō onomati tou kyriou ('moving freely in Jerusalem, speaking boldly in the name of the Lord'): eisporeuomenos kai ekporeuomenos (going in and out) is the Septuagintal idiom for active public ministry (the same phrase Ananias used to describe Jesus's ministry in Acts 1:21). Parrēsiazomenos (speaking boldly) is Acts' marker of Spirit-empowered public testimony.
29: elalei te kai sunezētei pros tous Hellēnistas ('he was speaking and debating with the Hellenistic Jews'): Saul's natural constituency — Greek-speaking diaspora Jews in Jerusalem — is the same community that martyred Stephen (Acts 6:9). The debate partners who killed Stephen now try to kill Saul; his conversion has transferred him from persecutor to persecuted within the same Hellenistic Jewish milieu.
30: katēgagon auton eis Kaisareian kai exapesteilan auton eis Tarson ('they brought him down to Caesarea and sent him off to Tarsus'): Tarsus is Saul's home city (Acts 22:3), a safe haven that also positions him in the region of Cilicia and Syria where Galatians 1:21 places his early ministry. The Caesarea → Tarsus route (by sea) removes him from the Jerusalem danger; God has arranged Saul's brief Jerusalem visit (15 days, Galatians 1:18) and now provides the escape route.
31: hē men oun ekklēsia kath' holēs tēs Ioudaias kai Galilaiias kai Samareias eichen eirēnēn oikodoumoumenē ('the church throughout Judea, Galilee, and Samaria had peace and was built up'): Luke's summary statement covers the three regions of Acts 1:8's mandate. Oikodomeō (build up) is the Pauline church-building metaphor (1 Corinthians 3:9-15; Ephesians 2:20-22); the church's growth in peacetime has the same architectural structure. tō phobō tou kyriou ... tē paraklēsei tou hagiou pneumatos: the twin modes of community formation — reverential fear and Spirit-encouragement.
32: egeneto de Petron dierchoumenon dia pantōn katelthein kai pros tous hagious tous katoikountas Lydda ('Peter went to visit the saints who lived in Lydda'): tous hagious (the saints/holy ones) is the covenant-community designation derived from OT qedoshim; it is Paul's standard address in his letters and appears here in Luke for what seems the first time in Acts. The Lydda-Joppa-Caesarea narrative (Acts 9:32-10:48) traces Peter's coastal journey toward the pivotal Cornelius encounter.
33: Iēsous Christos iatai se ('Jesus Christ heals you'): Peter does not invoke the name as a formula but makes a direct declarative statement — the healing is Jesus Christ's action, with Peter as the instrument. Strōson seautō ('make your bed/mat'): the practical instruction confirms the healing's completeness — not merely restored sensation but immediate functional capacity.
34: pantes hoi katoikountes Lydda kai ton Sarōna hoi tines epestrepsan epi ton kyrion ('all who lived in Lydda and Sharon turned to the Lord'): the geographic specificity (Lydda and the Sharon plain) grounds the mission in identifiable territory; these villages would later become significant in the Jewish-Christian communities of the second century. The mass turning (epestrepsan) precedes the Cornelius episode — the coastal plain is being prepared for the Gentile breakthrough.
35: mathētria tis ēn en Ioppē onomati Tabitha ('there was a disciple named Tabitha'): mathētria (female disciple) is a hapax in the NT — the only occurrence of the feminine form. Luke's use of it for Tabitha is deliberate: she is not merely a helper or patron but a disciple in her own right. The bilingual name (Dorkas in Greek = gazelle) shows her integration into both Jewish and Hellenistic communities. Her ergōn agathōn kai eleēmosynōn (good works and almsgiving) are the marks of active covenant-community life.
36: egeneto de en tais hēmerais ekeinais asthenēsasan autēn apothanein ('in those days she became ill and died'): the temporal marker en tais hēmerais ekeinais connects Tabitha's death to the Lydda mission, setting up Peter's proximity as providential. The preparation of her body (lousantes ethekan en hyperōō, washed and placed in the upper room) is Jewish burial preparation; the upper room is the same distinctive Jerusalem Christian meeting space (Acts 1:13; Acts 20:8).
37: aposteilantes duo andras pros auton parekaloun ('sending two men they urged him'): the two-man delegation mirrors the pattern of official mission (Acts 10:7; Luke 10:1); the urgency of their request — Peter is nearby, the death is recent — suggests they expected or hoped for resurrection, not merely comfort. The appeal to 'not delay in coming' (mē okneō dielthein heōs hēmōn) is the language of life-or-death urgency.
38: pasai hai chērai pareistēkeisan autō klaious ai kai epideiknymena chitōnas kai himatia ('all the widows stood around him weeping and showing tunics and cloaks'): the widows' display of Tabitha's work is both a testimony to her ministry and an appeal grounded in relationship. The clothing she made for them represents her eleēmosyne (almsgiving) made concrete; the community's grief is proportional to the loss of a specific, irreplaceable benefactor.
39: ekteinasa de Petros ta gonata proseuxato ('Peter knelt down and prayed'): Peter's kneeling prayer before the miracle reproduces the pattern of Jesus's prayer before raising Lazarus (John 11:41-42). The dependence on the Father is explicit: Peter does not command the resurrection but prepares through prayer. The contrast with Jesus's immediate word of command (Luke 8:54) is intentional — the apostle acts in Christ's name and power, not in his own authority.
40: Tabitha, anastēthi ('Tabitha, arise'): the Aramaic retention matches Jesus's Talitha koum at Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:41); the parallel is deliberate — Peter raising Tabitha is the apostolic continuation of Jesus's resurrection-ministry. aneblepsas kai idousa ton Petron anekathisen ('opening her eyes and seeing Peter, she sat up'): the sequence — eyes open, recognizes Peter, sits up — mirrors awakening from sleep, which is Acts' consistent resurrection imagery.
41: dous de autē cheira anestēsen autēn ('taking her by the hand he helped her up'): the raising-by-the-hand echoes Jesus's gesture with Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:41; Luke 8:54) and Peter's raising of the lame man (Acts 3:7). The bodily, physical touch is the sign of genuine resurrection — not a vision or spiritual experience but a hand grasped and a person lifted. Tois hagiois kai tais chērais: Peter presents her to both the whole community and the widows specifically — the most affected are the first to receive her back.
42: gnōston de egeneto kath' holēs tēs Ioppēs kai episteusan polloi epi ton kyrion ('this became known throughout Joppa and many believed in the Lord'): the Tabitha resurrection is the anchor event for the Joppa community; the same pattern of miracle → belief → community-expansion governs the Lydda and Sharon response (Acts 9:35). Joppa is the city where Jonah boarded the ship to flee; it becomes the city where Peter receives the vision that sends him to the Gentiles (Acts 10).
43: egeneto de hēmeras hikanas meinai en Ioppē para tini Simōni bursει ('he stayed in Joppa for some time with a tanner named Simon'): a tanner's work with animal hides made him ritually impure by Jewish standards; Peter's willingness to lodge with him is a first step toward the boundary-crossing that the Cornelius episode will complete. Luke's geographic and social placement of Peter here prepares the reader for the vision of unclean animals that will challenge Peter's purity-map in Acts 10.
